posts.title: Pop Peptides and the Evidence Gap: What Knoepfler's STAT Essay Gets Right About BPC-157 and GHK-Cu
posts.subtitle: A UC Davis scientist says Kennedy's reversal of FDA's 2023 peptide ban runs ahead of the trial data. The evidence on BPC-157 and GHK-Cu, and what PCAC will actually decide on July 23.
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.excerpt: A UC Davis scientist's STAT essay says Kennedy's peptide reversal runs ahead of trial data. What's actually published on BPC-157 and GHK-Cu, and what PCAC can do.
posts.metaDescription: What Paul Knoepfler's STAT essay gets right: BPC-157 and GHK-Cu lack the human trial data PCAC will weigh on July 23. Evidence breakdown for the seven peptides.
posts.keywords: BPC-157 evidence, GHK-Cu safety, FDA PCAC July 2026, 503A bulks list, RFK peptide policy, peptide compounding, Knoepfler STAT, peptide clinical trials, Category 2 peptides, KPV TB-500 MOTs-C DSIP Semax Epitalon

posts.title: FDA Sets July 23-24 PCAC Vote on Seven Peptides for the 503A Bulks List: BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTs-C, DSIP, Semax, Epitalon
posts.subtitle: FDA published the July 2026 PCAC agenda on April 15. Two dockets are open. Comments to FDA-2026-N-2979 must arrive by July 9 to reach the committee before the vote.
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.excerpt: FDA's July 23-24 PCAC will vote on seven peptides for the 503A Bulks List. Two dockets are open. July 9 is the cutoff for comments to reach the committee.
posts.metaDescription: FDA's July 23-24, 2026 PCAC will vote on BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTs-C, DSIP, Semax, and Epitalon for the 503A Bulks List. Comment by July 9 to reach the committee.

posts.title: Tirzepatide Linked to Fewer Cardiac Events After PCI and TAVR: Three Propensity-Matched Studies at SCAI 2026 and AACE 2026
posts.subtitle: Propensity-matched analyses of 86,000 patients in obesity-with-hypothyroidism, plus smaller cohorts after PCI and after transcatheter aortic valve replacement, all show tirzepatide outperforming GLP-1 comparators on hard endpoints.
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.excerpt: Three propensity-matched studies presented April 23-24 link tirzepatide to fewer deaths and major cardiovascular events versus GLP-1 comparators in PCI, TAVR, and obese hypothyroid populations.
posts.metaDescription: SCAI 2026 and AACE 2026 propensity-matched data show tirzepatide reduces cardiovascular events after PCI and TAVR and lowers 5-year mortality vs semaglutide.
posts.keywords: tirzepatide, dulaglutide, semaglutide, SCAI 2026, AACE 2026, PCI cardiovascular outcomes, TAVR outcomes, GLP-1 procedural cardiology, MACE, obesity

posts.title: Tirzepatide vs Semaglutide Cost-Effectiveness: SURMOUNT-5 Lifetime Model Shows $41,688 Per-Patient Savings
posts.subtitle: A peer-reviewed lifetime simulation built on the SURMOUNT-5 head-to-head trial finds tirzepatide dominant on cost and QALYs in U.S. obesity care without type 2 diabetes.
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.excerpt: A new lifetime simulation built on SURMOUNT-5 data finds tirzepatide saves $41,688 per patient and adds 0.506 QALYs versus semaglutide.
posts.metaDescription: Tirzepatide vs semaglutide cost-effectiveness: SURMOUNT-5-based lifetime model shows $41,688 savings, 0.506 QALY gain, fewer T2D and CVD cases per 1,000.

posts.title: FDA Opens Docket FDA-2025-N-6895 for July 2026 PCAC Peptide Review
posts.subtitle: Seven peptides go before the Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee on July 23–24. Here is what FDA is actually weighing — and what removal from Category 2 does not change.
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Federal Register notice and calendar marked July 2026 PCAC meeting beside labeled peptide vials (BPC-157, KPV, TB-500, MOTs-C, Semax, Epitalon) on a sterile laboratory bench
posts.excerpt: FDA opened docket FDA-2025-N-6895 ahead of the July 23–24, 2026 PCAC meeting on seven peptides. What is on the agenda and what stays unchanged for 503A compounding.
posts.metaDescription: FDA docket FDA-2025-N-6895 sets up the July 2026 PCAC review of seven peptides. What the committee will weigh, and what Category 2 removal does not change.

posts.title: FDA Import Alert 66-80: GLP-1 and Peptide Bulk API Now Detained at the Border
posts.subtitle: FDA activates Detention Without Physical Examination (DWPE) for semaglutide, tirzepatide, liraglutide, exenatide, dulaglutide, retatrutide, and certain peptide drug products — what it means for compounders, telehealth, and research-peptide supply
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: US customs inspector scanning a pharmaceutical shipping carton stamped DETAINED in red next to amber peptide API vials and an FDA Import Alert notice at a port of entry inspection table
posts.excerpt: FDA has updated Import Alert 66-80 to authorize Detention Without Physical Examination for bulk GLP-1 API (semaglutide, tirzepatide, liraglutide, exenatide, dulaglutide, retatrutide) and certain peptide drug products. The agency is moving enforcement upstream — choking off the compounded GLP-1 market at the port instead of chasing pharmacies and telehealth clinics one at a time.
posts.metaDescription: FDA Import Alert 66-80 activates Detention Without Physical Examination for bulk semaglutide, tirzepatide, and peptide APIs. What DWPE means, who’s in scope, and a compliance checklist for 503A/503B compounders and telehealth.

posts.title: SAB-142 Phase 1 Data: C-Peptide Preservation in Established Type 1 Diabetes
posts.subtitle: SAB Biotherapeutics reports all four treated adults preserved endogenous C-peptide at Day 120, with CGM time-in-range climbing from 73% to 85%
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Endocrinology researcher placing a continuous glucose monitor sensor on a Type 1 Diabetes clinical trial participant, next to a lab vial and a printed C-peptide preservation curve chart
posts.excerpt: At IDS 2026 on April 22, SAB Biotherapeutics reported that all four adults with established Type 1 Diabetes dosed with SAB-142 preserved endogenous C-peptide at Day 120, with mean CGM time-in-range climbing from 73% to 85%. A small but mechanistically coherent signal in a disease where C-peptide almost always declines.
posts.metaDescription: SAB-142 Phase 1 data (Apr 22, 2026): all four treated adults with established Type 1 Diabetes preserved endogenous C-peptide at Day 120; CGM time-in-range rose from 73% to 85%. Mechanistic signal from TIGIT+ Tconv cells.

posts.title: FDA’s Peptide ‘Rally’ Update: Supply Chain and Enforcement Are the Real Bottlenecks
posts.subtitle: FDA Law Blog (Post 2) explains why compliant API sourcing and marketing enforcement may matter more than Category 2 headlines
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Peptides and policy concept image used in PeptideKnow coverage of RFK and FDA peptide actions
posts.excerpt: A fresh follow-up from the FDA Law Blog (Apr 22, 2026) argues the biggest barrier to “peptides being legal again” isn’t just the PCAC calendar — it’s whether pharmacies can source pharmaceutical-grade peptide API (with COAs from FDA-registered establishments) and whether telehealth-style marketing triggers the same enforcement playbook FDA used against compounded GLP-1 promotions.
posts.metaDescription: FDA peptide compounding update (Apr 22, 2026): why compliant API sourcing and marketing enforcement may be the real bottlenecks — even if PCAC reviews peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500 for the 503A bulks list.

posts.title: FDA’s ‘Peptide Rally’: What Changed — and What Hasn’t (Yet)
posts.subtitle: A plain-English guide to Category 2 removal, the July 2026 PCAC hearing, and why formal rulemaking still matters for 503A compounding
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Peptides and policy concept image used in PeptideKnow coverage of RFK and FDA peptide actions
posts.excerpt: A new analysis from the FDA Law Blog explains why the FDA’s recent ‘Category 2 removal’ headlines don’t automatically make peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500 legal to compound. Here’s what the FDA actually scheduled (PCAC in July 2026), why notice-and-comment rulemaking still matters, and what patients and pharmacies should watch next.
posts.metaDescription: Did the FDA ‘legalize’ peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500? Not yet. Learn what Category 2 removal does (and doesn’t) mean, the July 23–24, 2026 PCAC timeline, and why formal rulemaking still controls 503A compounding access.

posts.title: FDA Sets July 2026 PCAC Hearing for BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, Semax, and More
posts.subtitle: What the FDA will review at the July 23–24 Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee meeting — and what it means for compounded peptides
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: FDA advisory committee meeting concept with peptide vials and regulatory documents
posts.excerpt: The FDA has published the agenda for its July 23–24, 2026 Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting, naming seven peptides—including BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, Semax, and Epitalon—for discussion as potential additions to the 503A bulks list. Here’s what’s on the agenda, what “uses evaluated” means, and what to watch before July.
posts.metaDescription: The FDA’s July 23–24, 2026 PCAC meeting will discuss BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, KPV, DSIP (emideltide), Semax, and Epitalon for possible inclusion on the 503A bulks list. Full breakdown of the agenda, deadlines, and implications for compounded peptides.

posts.title: BRP: Stanford's AI-Discovered Peptide That Could Replace Ozempic
posts.subtitle: A 12-amino-acid molecule found by AI targets only the brain's appetite center — no nausea, no muscle loss, no GI side effects
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Molecular peptide structure floating above a brain model in a neuroscience laboratory — representing BRP peptide discovery
posts.excerpt: Stanford researchers used an AI algorithm called Peptide Predictor to scan 20,000 human genes and discovered BRP — a naturally occurring 12-amino-acid peptide that reduces food intake by 50% in animal models, causes fat-specific weight loss, and produces none of the nausea, muscle loss, or GI side effects associated with Ozempic. Here's what the Nature paper actually shows, and why it matters.
posts.metaDescription: Stanford scientists used AI to discover BRP (BRINP2-Related Peptide), a naturally occurring 12-amino-acid peptide that suppresses appetite through a novel hypothalamic mechanism — with no nausea, no muscle loss, and no GI side effects seen in animal studies. Full analysis of the Nature paper, mechanism, animal data, and comparison to Ozempic.

posts.title: FDA Peptide Reclassification 2026: Which 12 Peptides Are Being Restored Under RFK Jr.
posts.subtitle: A comprehensive guide to the FDA's reversal on compounded peptides, the PCAC review timeline, and what it means for patients and providers
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: FDA building exterior with laboratory peptide vials representing the 2026 peptide reclassification decision
posts.excerpt: In April 2026, the FDA announced it would remove 12 peptides from its Category 2 list, reversing a sweeping 2023 ban that cut off patient access to compounds like BPC-157, TB-500, and Semax. Here's the full breakdown of what changed, which peptides are affected, and the timeline ahead.
posts.metaDescription: Complete guide to the 2026 FDA peptide reclassification. Learn which 12 peptides (BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, MOTS-C, more) are being restored, the PCAC review timeline, and what RFK Jr.'s MAHA movement means for compounding pharmacies and patient access.
posts.keywords: FDA peptide reclassification 2026, RFK peptides, peptide ban reversal, BPC-157 legal status, TB-500 FDA, compounding pharmacy peptides, PCAC meeting July 2026, Category 2 peptides, MAHA peptides, which peptides are banned

posts.title: How to Submit Public Comments on FDA Peptide Compounding (Docket FDA-2025-N-6895)
posts.subtitle: A step-by-step guide to making your voice heard before the July 2026 PCAC meeting deadline
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: A person typing a public comment letter on a laptop with a government building visible in the background, documents labeled 'Public Comments' and 'Proposed Regulation' on the desk
posts.excerpt: The FDA has opened docket FDA-2025-N-6895 for public comments on seven peptides — including BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, and Epitalon — ahead of the July 23–24, 2026 PCAC hearing. Here's exactly how to submit your comment, request an oral presentation, and make your voice count before the July 9 priority deadline.
posts.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to submitting public comments to FDA docket FDA-2025-N-6895 before the July 2026 PCAC meeting on peptide compounding. Covers BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, Epitalon, MOTs-C, KPV, and DSIP. Deadlines: July 9 (Committee) and July 22 (docket close).
